Two interview tracks

Self-paced SvelteKit guide 4 focused prep flows

Prepare for system design, low-level design, and DSA interviews in one place.

Study distributed systems architecture, low-level object design, and data structures and algorithms in separate guided flows, with mobile-friendly lessons, saved practice answers, and interactive drills.

Pick the flow that matches the interview in front of you, then work the modules in order so your answers sound structured instead of improvised.

Open HLD flow Open LLD flow Open DSA flow

Separate paths for architecture-heavy HLD rounds, object-design-heavy LLD rounds, and coding-focused DSA loops.

Organized into flow-specific sequences so the next step stays obvious.

Saved locally so you can move between modules without losing your place.

0 / 95 lessons complete Dedicated HLD and LLD landing pages Interactive practice labs with saved drafts
High-level design interview prep Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Session loop

Practice Frame, estimate, sketch, defend

Use the same four-part rhythm in every practice session.

The goal is not to collect diagrams. It is to build a repeatable answer shape that stays coherent under follow-up pressure.

Frame the user and the failure

Begin with the user journey, the request that matters most, and the failure you cannot afford. This keeps the design anchored to behavior instead of drifting into infrastructure trivia.

A system design answer becomes convincing when every later choice can be traced back to one explicit user promise.
Quantify the shape of the workload

Estimate read volume, write volume, object size, and growth curve before selecting storage, caching, or asynchronous pipelines. Numbers do not need to be perfect, but they should be directionally strong enough to eliminate unrealistic options.

Designing without rough numbers usually leads to generic diagrams that are hard to defend under follow-up questions.
Sketch one baseline before optimizing

Draw the simplest credible request path first. A clean baseline gives the interviewer something concrete to react to and gives you a stable place to introduce scale, durability, and resiliency improvements.

Good interview pacing comes from delaying optimizations until the first bottleneck is visible.
Deep dive where the trade-off is sharpest

Spend the extra time on the decision that most changes correctness, latency, or operational cost, such as replication strategy, fan-out design, or partitioning model.

Depth matters more than breadth once the baseline is clear; one defended decision is better than a dozen unexamined components.

HLD flow

High-level design interview prep

9 modules

Use this flow when the interview expects requirements framing, scale estimates, APIs, storage choices, bottleneck analysis, and operational trade-offs across distributed systems.

Best for Best for backend, platform, and senior product-engineering interviews that emphasize architecture under scale.
Cadence Follow the modules in order or jump into case studies once the core building blocks feel familiar.
Outcome You should be able to drive a whiteboard-style conversation from user needs to deep trade-off discussion without drifting into generic component lists.
  1. Focus 1 Requirements, estimation, and architecture baselines

    Start with requirements and workload shape, then defend the one architectural decision that most affects correctness, latency, or cost.

  2. Focus 2 Distributed systems building blocks such as caching, queues, replication, and partitioning

    Start with requirements and workload shape, then defend the one architectural decision that most affects correctness, latency, or cost.

  3. Focus 3 Product-shaped case studies that rehearse full interview narratives

    Start with requirements and workload shape, then defend the one architectural decision that most affects correctness, latency, or cost.

Open HLD roadmap Start with Foundations and estimation

LLD flow

Low-level design interview prep

4 modules

Use this flow when the interview focuses on classes, interfaces, workflows, patterns, testing seams, and how code structure adapts to changing requirements.

Best for Best for SDE-1 to senior engineer rounds that test object-oriented design, machine coding, and code-level architecture judgment.
Cadence Work module by module and code small examples alongside the lessons so the design vocabulary turns into implementation muscle memory.
Outcome You should be able to produce a clean class design quickly, justify pattern choices, and extend the design under interviewer follow-ups without rewriting everything.
  1. Focus 1 Prompt framing, invariants, and responsibility assignment

    Frame the scope first, sketch the smallest coherent object model, then explain how your contracts absorb follow-up requirements without collapsing into a god class.

  2. Focus 2 Object modeling, workflow orchestration, and change-friendly abstractions

    Frame the scope first, sketch the smallest coherent object model, then explain how your contracts absorb follow-up requirements without collapsing into a god class.

  3. Focus 3 Machine-coding execution, testing seams, concurrency, and scale follow-ups

    Frame the scope first, sketch the smallest coherent object model, then explain how your contracts absorb follow-up requirements without collapsing into a god class.

Open LLD roadmap Start with LLD foundations and prompt framing

DSA flow

Data structures and algorithms interview prep

4 modules

Use this flow when the interview expects coding, algorithm selection, complexity discussion, and live problem solving under follow-up questions.

Best for Best for coding screens, online assessments, phone interviews, and onsite rounds centered on data structures and algorithms.
Cadence Alternate between one concept-focused module and one mock-style module so pattern study turns into timed execution.
Outcome You should be able to identify common coding patterns quickly, explain trade-offs clearly, and complete structured mock rounds with less drift and fewer implementation mistakes.
  1. Focus 1 Core DSA patterns such as arrays, hash maps, trees, graphs, and dynamic programming

    Restate the problem, choose the pattern deliberately, defend the invariant or recurrence, then code the cleanest correct version before optimizing.

  2. Focus 2 Execution habits for live coding: invariants, complexity, tests, and follow-up handling

    Restate the problem, choose the pattern deliberately, defend the invariant or recurrence, then code the cleanest correct version before optimizing.

  3. Focus 3 Company-specific practice sets built from Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Apple question banks

    Restate the problem, choose the pattern deliberately, defend the invariant or recurrence, then code the cleanest correct version before optimizing.

Open DSA roadmap Start with DSA foundations and warm-ups

AI/ML flow

AI Engineer learning path

8 modules

Use this flow when you want to build or level up skills in machine learning, deep learning, large language models, AI agents, and production ML systems.

Best for Best for software engineers transitioning to AI/ML roles, data scientists moving to engineering, and AI engineers looking to fill knowledge gaps.
Cadence Progress module by module, building from ML fundamentals through advanced topics like agents and MLOps. Apply each module with a hands-on project.
Outcome You should be able to design, build, deploy, and monitor AI-powered applications from prototype through production, making informed tradeoffs between model quality, cost, latency, and safety.
  1. Focus 1 ML fundamentals, deep learning architectures, and model evaluation

    Start with foundations, build intuition for model training and evaluation, then specialize in LLMs, agents, or production ML systems based on your career goals.

  2. Focus 2 LLMs, prompt engineering, RAG pipelines, and AI agents

    Start with foundations, build intuition for model training and evaluation, then specialize in LLMs, agents, or production ML systems based on your career goals.

  3. Focus 3 MLOps, model serving, monitoring, and responsible AI practices

    Start with foundations, build intuition for model training and evaluation, then specialize in LLMs, agents, or production ML systems based on your career goals.

Open AI/ML roadmap Start with Machine learning foundations

HLD starting points

Three strong entry points into the high-level design flow.

01 5 lessons

Foundations and estimation

Build the habit of turning vague prompts into concrete requirements, scale assumptions, and explicit trade-offs.

Open module
02 5 lessons

Networking, edge, and request routing

Learn how requests find your system, how traffic is distributed, and where you terminate policy, caching, and security controls.

Open module
03 5 lessons

Application architecture and APIs

Choose service boundaries, communication styles, and coordination patterns that fit both the workload and the team operating the system.

Open module

What changes when this clicks

Answers stop sounding like component lists and start sounding like design decisions.

Requirement-first structure

You open with scope, workload, and correctness constraints instead of jumping straight to Kafka, caches, and databases.

Clear baseline architecture

You can explain one request path end to end before discussing scale levers, failure isolation, and regional expansion.

Sharper trade-off language

You get faster at defending choices around consistency, cost, complexity, and operational risk when the interviewer pushes deeper.

High-level design study modes

These guided tracks still map to the HLD curriculum when you want structured repetition.

Treat the map like a progression from framing to trade-offs. The best interview answers do not list components at random; they move from requirements and estimates into a clear baseline, then spend the remaining time defending the hardest decisions.

Study path

Interview sprint

5 detailed steps

Use the lesson checklists, module summaries, and case studies to rehearse the order of an interview answer in a compressed 7 to 10 day window.

Best for You already know the building blocks and need sharper pacing, better whiteboard structure, and cleaner trade-off narration.
Cadence Two focused sessions per day: one topic review and one timed case-study walkthrough.
What success looks like By the end of the sprint you should be able to open with requirements, estimate scale, name the baseline architecture, and justify one deep-dive choice without wandering.
  1. Step 1 Clarify the prompt

    Start each practice round by naming actors, core use cases, hard constraints, and what success means for the first version.

  2. Step 2 Estimate before designing

    Turn the prompt into peak QPS, storage growth, and latency budgets so every later choice feels grounded instead of improvised.

  3. Step 3 Draw one clean baseline

    Prefer a simple read path and write path that you can explain in under two minutes before layering in caches, queues, or replicas.

  4. Step 4 Deep dive into the highest-risk area

    Pick the bottleneck that most changes the architecture, such as fan-out, consistency, hot partitions, or global traffic management.

  5. Step 5 Close with trade-offs and operations

    End by naming failure modes, observability needs, and the next scaling lever so the answer sounds production-minded.

Study path

Deep understanding

5 detailed steps

Move module by module to build intuition for when each component is useful, where it breaks down, and what it costs to operate.

Best for You want durable intuition rather than memorized templates, especially for replication, partitioning, queues, caches, and consistency trade-offs.
Cadence Three to five lessons per week with time to redraw diagrams and explain the lesson back in your own words.
What success looks like You should leave each module with a mental model of the critical path, the main failure modes, and the reason one pattern beats another for a given workload.
  1. Step 1 Read for the mental model

    Focus on what problem the component solves, what assumptions it makes, and how it changes latency, durability, or coordination.

  2. Step 2 Study the diagram slowly

    Use the visual path to explain where requests enter, where state lives, and how data is replicated or recovered.

  3. Step 3 Review failure modes

    Ask what happens during partial outages, stale reads, retries, hot keys, or region failure so the lesson becomes operational knowledge.

  4. Step 4 Answer the interview prompts aloud

    Speaking through the prompt is where weak understanding shows up, so use the prompts as explanation drills instead of reading checks.

  5. Step 5 Mark complete only after recall

    A lesson is truly learned when you can reconstruct the idea, the trade-offs, and a concrete example without looking at the page.

Study path

System walkthrough practice

5 detailed steps

Use the case-study module to simulate whiteboard conversations and connect multiple lessons into one coherent end-to-end design.

Best for You need to practice stitching requirements, APIs, storage, scaling, and resilience into a single narrative instead of discussing topics in isolation.
Cadence One full case study every few days, followed by a short retrospective on what you skipped or overdesigned.
What success looks like You learn to transition between subsystems naturally, defend interfaces between them, and keep the conversation tied to user-facing goals.
  1. Step 1 Pick a product prompt with real constraints

    Choose something with a recognizable workload such as feeds, search, chat, ledgers, or link redirection so trade-offs feel concrete.

  2. Step 2 State assumptions before drawing

    Call out expected scale, freshness requirements, abuse risks, and whether the first version optimizes for speed of delivery or long-term scale.

  3. Step 3 Choose storage and traffic patterns

    Map each access pattern to the right storage model, caching layer, and edge strategy instead of defaulting everything to one database.

  4. Step 4 Add scaling and resilience controls

    Introduce queues, partitioning, replication, retries, and rate limits only where the workload justifies the operational cost.

  5. Step 5 Compare alternatives at the end

    Show maturity by naming the design you rejected, why it was tempting, and under what future conditions it would become the better choice.

Study path

Interactive answer drafting

5 detailed steps

Open the practice lab inside each lesson, save your answer locally, and move through prompts one step at a time like a mock interview.

Best for You learn best by writing. This path works well when you need repeatable answer templates and want to compare early drafts against stronger ones later.
Cadence Short, frequent reps: draft one prompt, save it, revisit it the next day, and tighten the structure rather than writing everything from scratch.
What success looks like Your answers become more deliberate: clearer opening assumptions, better component ordering, and stronger final trade-off summaries.
  1. Step 1 Open a lesson practice lab

    Start with a lesson you recently studied so the prompt tests recall instead of introducing a completely new idea.

  2. Step 2 Write the first draft quickly

    Aim for structure before polish: requirements, scale, architecture, bottleneck, and trade-offs are more important than perfect wording.

  3. Step 3 Save the answer and move on

    Treat the locally saved draft as a checkpoint, not a final submission, so you can compare how your thinking evolves over time.

  4. Step 4 Advance to the next prompt

    Switching between interview prompts forces you to generalize the lesson instead of memorizing a single canned explanation.

  5. Step 5 Return later and revise

    The second pass is where you usually add better trade-offs, cleaner interfaces, and stronger operational details.

How the map builds depth

Move from design vocabulary to product-shaped systems.

Build the foundations first

Start with requirements, estimation, latency budgets, CAP-style trade-offs, DNS, and load balancing. These lessons create the language you will use everywhere else, especially when interviewers push on scale, consistency, and failure handling.

Layer in state and coordination

Once the request path is clear, move into storage, caching, replication, queues, and distributed-systems topics. This is the middle of the map where most architectures succeed or fail because it determines where truth lives and how work is coordinated.

Practice product-shaped systems

Finish by applying the building blocks to feeds, chat, search, file delivery, payments, and the case studies. The goal is not to memorize a canonical answer, but to recognize recurring patterns and adapt them to the constraints of each product.

Helpful design books

Use these references to deepen the lessons, not replace practice.

Designing Data-Intensive Applications

Martin Kleppmann

Best for replication, consistency, storage engines, logs, streams, and the operational trade-offs behind distributed data systems.

Use it when you want deeper intuition for why databases, event streams, and coordination mechanisms behave the way they do.
System Design Interview

Alex Xu and Sahn Lam

Best for interview pacing, common product prompts, and learning how to structure a design answer under time pressure.

Use it alongside the case studies when you want to rehearse the shape of an interview conversation, not just the technology choices.
Fundamentals of Software Architecture

Mark Richards and Neal Ford

Best for architecture characteristics, trade-off analysis, and the vocabulary that helps you explain why a design is optimized for one quality attribute over another.

Use it when you want stronger language for balancing scalability, maintainability, deployability, cost, and team complexity.

Curriculum map

Modules and lesson progress

Each module adds a new design muscle: estimation, interfaces, storage, distributed coordination, reliability, security, and finally full case-study synthesis.

Module

Foundations and estimation

Build the habit of turning vague prompts into concrete requirements, scale assumptions, and explicit trade-offs.

  • Frame product requirements before picking components
  • Estimate traffic, storage, and throughput with quick math
  • Explain performance, scalability, consistency, and availability in interviewer-friendly language
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Networking, edge, and request routing

Learn how requests find your system, how traffic is distributed, and where you terminate policy, caching, and security controls.

  • Explain DNS, CDNs, and load balancers as separate layers
  • Choose between L4 and L7 routing based on needed controls
  • Use edge layers to reduce latency and shield origin services
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Application architecture and APIs

Choose service boundaries, communication styles, and coordination patterns that fit both the workload and the team operating the system.

  • Select application decomposition patterns intentionally
  • Understand service discovery, coordination, and API trade-offs
  • Handle synchronous and real-time communication without hiding costs
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Data storage and indexing

Match storage engines and data models to access patterns, write volume, consistency needs, and query flexibility.

  • Explain relational and NoSQL trade-offs clearly
  • Choose replication, partitioning, and indexing strategies
  • Recognize when one workload needs multiple storage systems
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Reduce latency, smooth bursts, and survive failures by placing the right work in caches, queues, and resilient control loops.

  • Use cache layers and invalidation strategies responsibly
  • Choose asynchronous patterns to absorb bursty work
  • Design retries, idempotency, and back pressure without making incidents worse
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Security, multi-region, and operations

Design systems that are safe to expose, recoverable during disasters, and maintainable by real teams over time.

  • Apply practical authentication, authorization, and data protection patterns
  • Reason about multi-region architecture and disaster recovery
  • Include deployment, capacity, and cost controls in designs
4 lessons 0/4 complete

Module

Distributed systems building blocks

Go deeper on coordination, partitioning, probabilistic techniques, and workflow patterns that appear repeatedly in the primer and in senior-level interviews.

  • Explain the building blocks behind partitioning, coordination, and large-scale data movement
  • Recognize when to use quorums, hashing, probabilistic structures, and sagas
  • Connect these lower-level concepts back to product-facing system designs
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Common product design patterns

Practice recurring architectures that appear across many interview prompts and real-world products.

  • Recognize reusable patterns behind common product families
  • Explain fan-out, indexing, storage, and notification decisions
  • Use these patterns as building blocks in case studies
5 lessons 0/5 complete

Module

Case studies and interview drills

Work through the full primer-inspired set of product and architecture drills, from redirect services and paste storage to finance aggregation, timelines, and ranking pipelines.

  • Practice moving from requirements and estimates to a full design quickly
  • Cover the complete set of high-signal primer case studies in one interview-ready module
  • Explain bottlenecks, scaling steps, failure modes, and operational trade-offs with confidence
9 lessons 0/9 complete

Module

LLD foundations and prompt framing

Learn how to turn a machine-coding or object-design prompt into bounded requirements, clean responsibilities, and explicit object contracts.

  • Clarify scope, actors, and invariants before sketching classes
  • Model responsibilities so each type owns one coherent reason to change
  • Choose interfaces and APIs that survive follow-up requirements
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Object modeling and domain relationships

Practice identifying entities, value objects, aggregates, and collaborations so your designs stay cohesive as requirements grow.

  • Separate identity-bearing entities from immutable values
  • Choose composition, association, or inheritance based on behavior
  • Keep state and workflows aligned with domain boundaries
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Extensibility and common LLD patterns

Use patterns as decision tools, not cargo cults, so your design stays open to change without drowning in abstraction.

  • Apply core patterns only when they solve a real axis of change
  • Use dependency inversion to isolate volatile collaborators
  • Keep pattern-heavy answers readable under interview time pressure
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling

Rehearse how to present class design, write extensible code under time pressure, and adapt cleanly when the interviewer adds new requirements.

  • Structure machine-coding answers so core classes appear quickly
  • Design for testability, concurrency, and follow-up changes
  • Narrate trade-offs while keeping the implementation incremental
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

DSA foundations and warm-ups

Start with the patterns that dominate coding screens: arrays, strings, linked lists, trees, and the first layer of dynamic programming.

  • Recognize common coding-round patterns quickly from the prompt shape
  • Write a correct first-pass solution before optimizing under follow-up pressure
  • Explain invariants, edge cases, and complexity clearly while coding
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Core patterns for coding rounds

Sharpen the medium-to-hard patterns that appear in stronger interview loops: sliding windows, recursion, graphs, and optimization-heavy DP.

  • Recognize the prompt signals that suggest windows, recursion trees, or memoization
  • Talk through state transitions cleanly before writing implementation details
  • Handle follow-ups that change constraints without restarting from zero
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Company-specific DSA rounds

Practice question clusters that resemble Amazon, Google, and other large-company coding loops, using the company datasets directly.

  • Get comfortable switching between general patterns and company-specific question mixes
  • Rehearse explaining trade-offs in the style commonly expected in major tech interviews
  • Use company sets to simulate tighter, more targeted mock rounds
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Mock loops and progression

Use warm-up, cross-company, and stretch sets to simulate a full DSA study progression from easy reps to harder follow-up-heavy rounds.

  • Build a repeatable practice cadence across easy, medium, and hard problem sets
  • Blend general and company datasets into realistic mixed mock rounds
  • Track which patterns still break down under time pressure
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Machine learning foundations

Build core intuition for supervised and unsupervised learning, feature engineering, model evaluation, and the mathematical underpinnings every AI engineer needs.

  • Understand linear algebra, calculus, and probability concepts relevant to ML
  • Apply classical ML algorithms: regression, classification, clustering
  • Evaluate models with proper validation techniques and metrics
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Deep learning

Neural network architectures from feed-forward networks through CNNs, RNNs, and transformers, with hands-on training using PyTorch and TensorFlow.

  • Build and train neural networks from scratch and with frameworks
  • Understand CNN, RNN, and transformer architectures and their applications
  • Apply transfer learning and fine-tuning to accelerate model development
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Large language models and NLP

From tokenization and embeddings to LLM pre-training, fine-tuning, and deployment for production NLP systems.

  • Understand how LLMs are trained, fine-tuned, and served at scale
  • Apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (LoRA, QLoRA, PEFT)
  • Design NLP pipelines for classification, summarization, and generation tasks
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Prompt engineering and RAG

Systematic prompt design, retrieval-augmented generation pipelines, and techniques for grounding LLM outputs in verified knowledge.

  • Design effective prompts using few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured techniques
  • Build end-to-end RAG pipelines that ground LLM responses in external knowledge
  • Evaluate and optimize retrieval quality and generation accuracy
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

AI agents and autonomous systems

Design, build, and deploy AI agents that reason, plan, use tools, and accomplish multi-step tasks autonomously.

  • Understand agent architectures: ReAct, plan-and-execute, and multi-agent systems
  • Implement tool use, memory, and planning capabilities in LLM-powered agents
  • Evaluate agent reliability, safety, and cost in production deployments
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

MLOps and production AI

End-to-end ML lifecycle management: experiment tracking, model serving, monitoring, CI/CD for ML, and infrastructure at scale.

  • Design ML pipelines from training through deployment and monitoring
  • Implement model serving with appropriate latency, cost, and reliability tradeoffs
  • Build monitoring systems that detect model drift and performance degradation
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI

Bias detection, fairness metrics, explainability, regulatory compliance, and building trustworthy AI systems.

  • Identify and mitigate bias in training data and model outputs
  • Implement explainability and interpretability for stakeholder trust
  • Navigate AI regulations (EU AI Act, GDPR) and organizational governance
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Data engineering for AI

Building robust data pipelines, managing training datasets at scale, and ensuring data quality for production ML systems.

  • Design data pipelines that handle ingestion, transformation, and validation at scale
  • Manage training datasets with proper versioning, lineage, and quality controls
  • Build feature stores that maintain consistency between training and serving
2 lessons 0/2 complete

Sources and attribution

Selected diagrams and topic framing were adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin. Selected diagrams originate from The System Design Primer and are licensed under CC BY 4.0.
